text: The surgeon performs an initial inspection of the peritoneal cavity through a single site multiport laparoscopic approach for a right hemicolectomy. A 3.5 cm incision is made along the umbilicus, and three trocars are placed: one 10 mm and two 5 mm.

The surgeon positions the transverse colon upwards and lifts the ileum to create a retroperitoneal tunnel behind the cecum and ascending colon. Dissection begins at the root of the mesentery, entering the avascular plane between the mesentery and retroperitoneum. The right gonadal vessel is identified, and the lateral attachments of the cecum and ascending colon remain intact. The right ureter is identified, and the peritoneal incision extends to the root of the mesentery.

The surgeon dissects the avascular plane, clearly visualizing the right gonadal vessels. The left hand instrument pushes the colon cranially to stretch the plane, facilitating dissection. The right ureter is identified and preserved, with the layer over it intact, ensuring no injury to the ureter, gonadal vessel, or duodenum.

The surgeon creates a tunnel above the duodenum, revealing the head of the pancreas. The mesenteric attachment of the right colon is lifted, and a gauze piece is placed for identification. The duodenum is clearly visible as the surgeon dissects tissue around it. An incision is made along the superior mesenteric pedicle, with prominent vessels visible due to prior dissection.

The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic vessel, dissecting multiple lymph nodes towards the specimen side. The nodes are removed from the origin of the ileocolic pedicle. With the retroperitoneal dissection completed, the vessel is lifted, allowing dissection behind it. The artery and vein are individually clipped using a Fiamam disposable clip applicator, with double clips applied. The vein is divided using the Fiamam ligasure, followed by the division of the artery.

The surgeon lifts the pedicle and enters the dissected area, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) clearly. A gauze piece aids in identifying the retroperitoneal dissected area. The surgeon proceeds to identify the middle colic vessel, noting the absence of a right colic vessel in this case. The right and left branches of the middle colic artery are visible, with the origin of the middle colic artery scrutinized. The right branch of the middle colic artery is skeletonized, clipped, and divided using a ligature coagulation device.

The surgeon separates the mesentery from the duodenum and the head of the pancreas using a 30-degree 10mm scope and two 5mm instruments. The proximal transverse colon is completely mobilized, and the lateral attachment of the right side colon is divided, with a gauze piece aiding in the process.

The surgeon maintains the layer over the Gerota's fascia in the dissected area. The small bowel mesentery is prepared along the line of division of the ileocolic vessel, and the transverse colon is also prepared. The head of the pancreas is clearly visible in the infracolic area. The specimen is removed, and an ileotransverse anastomosis is performed using a stapler. No drain is placed, and the final view of the surgery is shown.
